text: Heat adaptation has a moderately beneficial effect on exercise capacity and performance in the heat irrespective of the specific regimen used.
population: endurance athletes and recreationally active individuals

text: Heat adaptation regimens lasting longer than 14 days produce greater physiological and perceptual adaptations than shorter protocols.
population: endurance athletes and recreationally active individuals

text: Heat adaptation regimens lasting fewer than 14 days still induce many beneficial physiological and perceptual adaptations and improve exercise performance in the heat.
population: endurance athletes and recreationally active individuals

text: The most common effective heat adaptation protocol duration is 7-14 days using a controlled work-rate approach.
population: endurance athletes and recreationally active individuals

text: Heat adaptation has a moderate-to-large beneficial effect on lowering core body temperature before and during exercise, maintaining cardiovascular stability, and improving heat-loss pathways such as sweat rate and skin blood flow.
population: endurance athletes and recreationally active individuals

text: Heat adaptation may reduce oxygen consumption during exercise, improve glycogen sparing, increase power output at lactate threshold, and reduce lactate concentrations during exercise in the heat.
population: endurance athletes and recreationally active individuals

text: Heat adaptation improves perceived ratings of exertion and thermal sensation during subsequent exercise in the heat.
population: endurance athletes and recreationally active individuals
